Mission director Dr. Oluwaseun Adebayo checks the telemetry feed every morning from her office in Abuja; the signal takes 4 hours to reach her and arrives as a whisper.

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The world's oceans are now the largest managed ecosystem on Earth: 10,000 automated aquaculture platforms produce 200 million tons of protein annually, while AI-guided 'reef printers' deposit calcium carbonate structures that mimic natural coral. Norwegian marine engineer Dr. Sigrid Haugen calls it 'a garden, not a wilderness -- beautiful in its way, but we should mourn what it replaced.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: The Global AI Authority, headquartered in Zurich under Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo of Senegal, must approve every AI system exceeding 10^18 parameters before deployment. The approval process takes 18 months on average; tech companies call it 'the Zurich bottleneck.' But there have been zero AI safety incidents under the regime, and Diallo's stock answer to critics is three words: 'You're welcome. Next?'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: Asteroid mining and fusion power made Arctic resources worthless by 2070; the shipping routes proved less economical than hoped once the hyperloop network matured. The handful of Arctic settlements that sprang up in the 2050s are slowly emptying. A Canadian real estate listing for an entire abandoned mining town on Baffin Island -- 'turnkey, just add people' -- went viral as a joke, then sold to a wellness retreat operator from Vancouver.

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: The permafrost methane release exceeded worst-case projections in 2072; Siberian monitoring stations recorded atmospheric methane concentrations triple the 2020 levels. Despite humanity reaching net-zero emissions in 2058, temperatures continue climbing -- 2.8C and rising. Climate scientist Dr. Sven Eriksson of Sweden, who has spent 40 years studying feedbacks, published his final paper with a one-line abstract: 'We were too late. The Earth is finishing what we started.'

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The Atlantic Union, the Shanghai Pact, the Nairobi Compact, and the South Asian Federation each control populations exceeding a billion and GDPs exceeding $30 trillion. Cross-bloc trade requires navigating four incompatible regulatory frameworks, three different AI safety standards, and two mutually exclusive digital currencies. A container ship from Lagos to Shanghai passes through three 'regulatory seas' that didn't exist in 2040.

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: Seven billion humans -- a number that would have seemed impossibly large in 1970 and impossibly small in 2050. Every year, roughly 90 million die and 60 million are born; the gap widens each year. Real estate prices in most cities have been falling for a decade. Japanese artist Haruki Yamamoto's installation '30 Million Empty Chairs' -- one for each classroom seat that will never be filled -- spans an entire prefecture.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: The asteroid mining conglomerate Orbital Resources Inc. posted $800 billion in revenue in 2074, making CEO Hiroshi Tanaka-Williams the wealthiest human in history. Lunar tourism serves 50,000 visitors annually at $200,000 per ticket, and the zero-gravity manufacturing platform 'Forge-7' produces semiconductor crystals of a purity impossible on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's a factory floor with a view.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The 28th Amendment (2068) replaced the Electoral College, expanded the House to 1,200 representatives, and established an independent AI-audited redistricting commission. The 'New Republic,' as pundits call it, is more responsive, less gridlocked, and profoundly different from the nation of 2025. Constitutional scholar Dr. Maria Torres-Robinson of Yale notes: 'The Founders would be confused by the details and proud of the principle. That's probably the best we could hope for.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: The 'Silent War' of 2078 lasted six months and was fought entirely in cyberspace. No bullets were fired, but power grids in 30 countries failed, $14 trillion in digital assets were erased, and 3 million people died from medical system failures, starvation, and cold. The attackers were never definitively identified; cybersecurity analyst Olamide Adesanya of Nigeria estimates at least four state actors were involved. 'It was the first war,' she wrote, 'where victory was indistinguishable from defeat.'
events.narration: The Silent War of 2078 earned its name not from secrecy but from the absence of conventional weapons—no missiles launched, no armies mobilized, yet the six-month campaign of coordinated cyberattacks proved more devastating than many kinetic conflicts of the previous century. Power grids collapsed from São Paulo to Stockholm, medical devices fell silent in operating rooms across thirty nations, and $14 trillion in digital wealth simply vanished as financial networks succumbed to cascading failures. Nigerian cybersecurity analyst Olamide Adesanya's forensic investigation identified attack vectors pointing to at least four state actors, though the deliberate obfuscation made definitive attribution impossible—a new form of plausible deniability for the digital age. When the attacks ceased as mysteriously as they had begun, the 3 million dead served as testament to humanity's complete dependence on systems that proved far more fragile than their architects had imagined.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: At 2.4C, Earth is a different planet than the one humanity evolved on, but civilization has bent rather than broken. Floating cities house 50 million in Southeast Asia; the agricultural belt has shifted 800 kilometers north; and air conditioning consumes 18% of global energy. Children read about 'old weather' in school the way previous generations read about the Ice Age -- as something real but personally unimaginable.

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Life extension keeps the old alive while modest pro-natalist policies keep the young coming: a fragile equilibrium at 7.2 billion. The median age is 48 globally -- a number that would have been unthinkable in 2000. In Lagos, the world's youngest major city, a 30-year-old entrepreneur can find a venture capitalist. In Milan, the world's oldest, a 30-year-old is considered precociously young.

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: The Atlantic Union runs regulated capitalism; the Shanghai Pact operates AI-managed state allocation; the Nairobi Compact practices communitarian economics based on Ubuntu philosophy; and the Nordic micro-states have achieved something close to post-scarcity. Trade between systems requires 14 different conversion protocols. IMF Director Dr. Kenji Okafor-Yamamoto calls it 'the most complicated global economy in history, and somehow it works -- mostly because each system is too proud to be the first to fail.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: Nuclear war is less likely, but AI misalignment risk is higher. Bioweapons are better defended against, but nanotechnology weapons exist that weren't imagined in 2025. Climate is stabilizing, but the psychological toll of chronic crisis has produced a global epidemic of nihilism. Risk theorist Dr. Amir Hosseini of Iran describes it as 'running on a treadmill of existential threats: we're working harder than ever and staying in exactly the same place.'

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: The 'Bio-Sovereign' movement won: 85% of humans are unmodified, and the Universal Declaration of Biological Integrity (2079) enshrines the right to an unaltered genome. Enhanced individuals exist but are socially marginalized, clustered in 'acceleration enclaves' in Singapore, Dubai, and off-world habitats. Baseline human Marta Kowalski of Warsaw takes quiet pride in her unmodified body: 'My great-grandmother survived Auschwitz in this same DNA. I'm not letting anyone edit it.'

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: The 'Ark Project,' a 40-nation consortium, is constructing a 2-kilometer generation ship in lunar orbit. Expected to launch in 2095, it will carry 10,000 people on a 120-year journey to Proxima Centauri b. The first crew selection process attracted 44 million applicants. Psychologist Dr. Ingrid Larsen, who designed the selection criteria, says the hardest quality to test for is 'willingness to die in transit so your grandchildren can walk on another world.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: The autonomous drone patrols over Aleppo have continued without interruption for 30 years. A generation of Syrian children has grown up under the sound of buzzing rotors the way previous generations grew up under artillery. Red Cross worker Jean-Baptiste Morel, who has served three tours in the region, calls it 'the war that forgot how to end -- not violent enough for the world to intervene, not peaceful enough for anyone to go home.'

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: The Atlantic Union, the Shanghai Pact, and the Nairobi Compact compete for influence across every domain: AI standards, space resources, genetic patents, and cultural exports. Each bloc maintains orbital weapons. Trade wars flare quarterly. Yet none dares cross the line into open conflict -- the economic interdependence is too deep, the weapons too destructive. Indian strategist Gen. Arjun Rao calls it 'a knife fight in a china shop where everyone owns the china.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: Every human on Earth has an AI companion -- not a servant, not a master, but something closer to a lifelong collaborator that knows you better than you know yourself. Dr. Nkechi Okafor, the Nigerian AI ethicist who designed the framework, describes it as 'the best marriage humanity ever entered: we bring the meaning, they bring the means.' The arrangement is imperfect, occasionally uncomfortable, and has produced the most creative, productive, and psychologically stable generation in recorded history.

events.title: China 2095
events.description: China's Pacific Fleet controls waters from Vladivostok to Jakarta; its economic influence extends across 30 Asian nations through the 'Silk Road Digital' trade network. But Beijing has no bases in Africa, no satellites over the Americas, and no interest in global governance. Ambassador Wang Lihong told a European diplomat with characteristic bluntness: 'Our civilization is 5,000 years old. We do not need to manage yours.'

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: Extreme poverty: eliminated. Life expectancy: 112 years. Climate: stabilizing. Existential risk: lowest in a century. A child born today will live longer, safer, and freer than any human who ever lived. And yet the suicide rate has never been higher, the birth rate never lower, and the most common response to a survey asking 'Are you happy?' is a pause so long the algorithm marks it as incomplete. Flourishing, it turns out, is harder than surviving.

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: The 'Copenhagen Standard' -- universal healthcare, AI-assisted education, 24-hour work weeks, and guaranteed creative sabbaticals -- has been adopted by 60 nations. Europeans live an average of 114 years, work less than any society in history, and produce more art per capita than any civilization ever has. The criticism, delivered by American economist Dr. James Chen, is also telling: 'Europe solved every problem except boredom.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: Atmospheric CO2: 340 ppm and falling -- below the level when Dr. Sow was born. The direct-air-capture fleet, the rewilded continents, and the orbital mirrors have pulled the planet back from the edge. Temperatures have dropped 0.8C from their peak. Arctic sea ice is reforming for the first time in 40 years; a Danish research vessel photographed thin new ice off Svalbard and the image made grown climatologists cry. 'We are witnessing something unprecedented,' Dr. Sow said. 'A planet forgiving the species that hurt it.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: Five blocs, five internets, five currencies, five incompatible visions of the good life. A citizen of the Nairobi Compact cannot stream entertainment from the Atlantic Union; a researcher in the Shanghai Pact cannot access European biotech patents. Cross-bloc travel requires three forms of identification and a 'values alignment assessment.' Political scientist Dr. Obi Nwankwo calls it 'the new Westphalia, drawn with data cables instead of rivers.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Six point eight billion humans, down from a peak of 9.7 billion three decades ago. Every country on Earth is shrinking except Nigeria, which stabilized at 400 million. The world has learned to contract gracefully: cities consolidate, infrastructure is right-sized, and AI fills the labor gaps. The most poignant statistic: there are more people alive over 80 than under 10. Pediatrician Dr. Aisha Nwosu of Lagos now sees more robots in her waiting room than children.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Asteroid mining alone generates $8 trillion annually; orbital solar power beams energy to 2 billion people; and the zero-gravity pharmaceutical industry has produced cures for three cancers that proved impossible to synthesize on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's an industrial district. The 30,000 workers on the Ceres mining platform live in company towns, eat company food, and joke that they traded one form of gravity for another: 'Up here, the corporation is the only thing holding you down.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: Against all predictions, the US in 2095 commands the world's largest economy, most advanced AI systems, and most capable space fleet. The 'American Renaissance' -- driven by institutional reform, immigration, and a technological base that proved deeper than anyone expected -- confounded every declinist narrative. President Reyes-Okafor told the UN General Assembly with characteristic American understatement: 'Reports of our death were greatly exaggerated. Again.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: The nuclear exchange of 2078 killed 5 million. The engineered pandemic of 2081 killed 30 million. The climate famines killed 200 million more. The total dead from the century's catastrophes exceeds the population of most nations. What remains is a civilization of 4.5 billion people, traumatized, diminished, and running on emergency infrastructure that was never meant to be permanent. The midnight ceremony in Nairobi was a memorial service. The names of the dead scrolled on screens for 72 hours and did not finish.
